Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: We are given a POWL model with potential anomalies

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: The model defines a loop between Evaluate E and Approve P activities This loop allows for multiple evaluations and approvals which might indicate that a claim can be evaluated and approved multiple times

Name: Validation - IND
Text: However the loop structure in POWL using Operator LOOP typically has a do part and a redo part In the code it is defined as E P which means the loop body is E followed by P and then it can either exit or repeat

Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: There is an XOR exclusive choice between Notify Customer N and a silent transition skip This means that the process may skip the notification step

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: The partial order structure defines edges R to A A to loop loop to xor However it also adds an edge A to C This edge allows the Close Claim C activity to happen after Assign Adjuster A without going through the loop and the xor

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: The model does not have an edge from xor to C so it is possible that C is executed concurrently with the loop and xor or even before them

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: The loop between E and P might exist because the business process allows for re evaluation and re approval

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Alternatively it might be a modeling error where the modeler intended to have a sequence of E then P but accidentally made it a loop

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: The XOR that skips notification might be due to certain claims not requiring customer notification or it might be an oversight in the model or a technical error in the workflow system

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: The direct edge from A to C might be due to a change in business rules where some claims can be closed immediately after assignment or it might be a modeling error or due to inadequate constraints in the process modelers tool

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: The lack of an edge from xor to C might be an oversight meaning that the process model does not enforce that closing the claim happens after the notification or skip

Name: Validation - PE
Text: We can check for claims that have multiple E and or P events Specifically we can look for claims where the activity Evaluate Claim appears more than once or where Approve Claim appears more than once

Name: Validation - PE
Text: We can check for claims that do not have a Notify Customer event and in particular claims that have been closed but do not have a Notify Customer event

Name: Validation - PE
Text: We want to find claims that were closed without having an evaluation or approval or claims that have an Assign Adjuster event and then a Close Claim event without any Evaluate Claim or Approve Claim events in between

Name: Validation - PE
Text: We can check for claims where the Close Claim event occurs before the Notify Customer event if notification occurs

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - IND
Text: We can also look at the event log to see if there are any patterns that match the anomalous paths

Name: Validation - PE
Text: If we find many instances then the anomalies might be intentional or at least tolerated If we find few or none then the anomalies might be modeling errors that do not reflect reality

Name: Validation - IND
Text: The actual activity labels in the database might be different from the single letters used in the model We have to adjust the queries to match the actual labels in the claim events table

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - IND
Text: We can also consider joining with the claims table to get more context such as the claim type amount and to see if the anomalies are associated with specific types of claims
